data:
This page demonstrates the assembly layout type with an interactive 3D model viewer.
The STEP file below was exported from the KiCad PCB by KiBot during CI.

data.about-this-example#About this example:
This page demonstrates the board layout type, which combines:
An interactive KiCanvas schematic/PCB viewer (above) A 3D render gallery (when renders are available) An interactive BOM viewer (when iBOM is available) Custom markdown content (this section) The KiCad files shown here are from the Arduino Uno Shield template included with KiCad.
In a real project, 3D renders and the interactive BOM are generated automatically by KiBot during CI.

data: This page demonstrates how to compose pages from Markdown using the call-partial shortcode — no custom HTML templates needed.
data.diffs-embedded-pdf#Diffs (embedded PDF):
Arduino_Uno Diff_pcb.Pdf ⛶ Your browser doesn't support embedded PDFs. Click here to download "Arduino_Uno Diff_pcb.Pdf".
Arduino_Uno Diff_sch.Pdf ⛶ Your browser doesn't support embedded PDFs. Click here to download "Arduino_Uno Diff_sch.Pdf".
data.how-it-works#How it works:
Instead of using a fixed layout type like board or assembly, you can insert any partial directly in your Markdown:
{{< call-partial "kicanvas.html" >}} {{< call-partial "gallery.html" >}} {{< call-partial "ibom.html" >}} {{< call-partial "assembly.html" >}} {{< call-partial "bom.html" >}} {{< call-partial "diffs.html" >}} {{< call-partial "diffs-pdf.html" >}} {{< call-partial "download-list.html" >}} This lets you mix and match components freely, add your own content between them, and control the order.
data.why-use-this#Why use this?: Full control over page structure and content order No Go/HTML knowledge required — pure Markdown Mix components with your own prose, tables, images, etc. Works with any partial: gallery.html, kicanvas.html, ibom.html, assembly.html

data: This page demonstrates Hextra shortcodes available in any content page — useful for assembly guides, design notes, and project documentation.
data.connector-placement#Connector Placement:
The Arduino Uno shield template places all pin headers along the board edges to match the standard Arduino form factor:
Connector Pins Function J1 1×8 Digital I/O 0–7 J2 1×6 Analog inputs A0–A5 J3 1×10 Digital I/O 8–13 + GND/AREF
data.mounting-holes#Mounting Holes: Four M3.2 mounting holes match the Arduino Uno board-to-board spacing. Use 11mm standoffs for proper clearance.
data.order-pcbs#Order PCBs: Upload Gerber files to your preferred fabricator. Recommended: 1.6mm FR4, HASL finish, green solder mask.
data.solder-smd-components#Solder SMD compone: Start with the smallest components (0402 passives), then ICs. Use solder paste and hot air or reflow oven.
data.solder-through-hole-connectors#Solder th: Install pin headers last — they’re tallest and would block access to SMD pads.
data.tabs-variant-comparison#Tabs (Variant Co:
2-layer4-layer 2-layer stackup — suitable for simple shields with low-speed signals.
Layer Usage Top Signal + components Bottom Ground plane Cost: ~$5 for 5 boards (JLCPCB)
4-layer stackup — recommended for high-speed or mixed-signal designs.
Layer Usage Top Signal + components Inner 1 Ground plane Inner 2 Power plane Bottom Signal + components Cost: ~$15 for 5 boards (JLCPCB)
data.test#Test: Power up with current-limited supply. Check 3.3V and 5V rails before connecting to Arduino.

data:
This page demonstrates the release layout type. In a real project with tagged releases, it shows:
A link to the GitHub release Download links for Gerbers, BOM, schematics (PDF) Visual diffs of schematic/PCB changes against the previous release All assets are discovered automatically by the CI workflow.
